event: The Number of the Beast is 666
symbols: great red dragon with multiple heads — Satan, the ancient serpent, the accuser (Revelation 12:9)
beast with horns below — the beast from the sea, representing worldly power in service of evil (Revelation 13:1)
fiery crown or radiance around the dragon — the counterfeit glory of Satan who masquerades as an angel of light
stars in the background — the third of the stars of heaven swept down by the dragon's tail (Revelation 12:4)
monstrous physicality — the grotesque reality of evil unmasked, stripped of all seductive beauty
description: A visionary watercolor by William Blake depicting two monstrous beings against a dark, star-speckled background. The upper figure is an enormous muscular humanoid creature with bat-like wings spread wide, multiple crowned heads radiating a fiery glow, and a thick serpentine body. Its posture is one of domination and menace, looming over the lower figure. Below, a pale, bloated beast with a grotesque grinning face and horns crouches on a reddish surface, one clawed hand raised. The beast has a lamb-like or lion-like quality twisted into something repulsive.
theological_description: William Blake's illustration of Revelation 13 brings to vivid, terrifying life the apocalyptic vision of John. The great red dragon of Revelation 12:3 -- 'a great red dragon, with seven heads and ten horns, and on his heads seven diadems' -- looms above, identified throughout Revelation as 'that ancient serpent, who is called the devil and Satan' (Revelation 12:9). Below him crouches the beast from the sea (Revelation 13:1-2), to whom the dragon gives 'his power and his throne and great authority.' This beast demands worship and is associated with the infamous number 666 (Revelation 13:18): 'Let the one who has understanding calculate the number of the beast, for it is the number of a man.' Blake's genius lies in making visible the inner nature of these beings: not attractive or seductive (as evil often appears in the world) but grotesque and repulsive. The dragon's counterfeit radiance parodies the glory of God, fulfilling Paul's warning that 'even Satan disguises himself as an angel of light' (2 Corinthians 11:14). The cowering beast below, despite its ferocity, is ultimately subservient to the dragon, illustrating the parasitic and derivative nature of all evil -- it cannot create, only corrupt. The Book of Revelation promises that both the dragon and the beast will be 'thrown into the lake of fire' (Revelation 20:10), making this scene not a vision of final triumph for evil but a disclosure of its true nature before its ultimate defeat.

scripture_refs.reason: The beast from the sea, the number 666, and the worship demanded by the beast -- the direct subject of the painting.
The great red dragon with seven heads and ten horns, identified as Satan -- the upper figure in the composition.
The ultimate fate of the dragon and beast thrown into the lake of fire -- the eschatological context of the scene.
Satan disguises himself as an angel of light -- the counterfeit radiance of the dragon's crowned heads.
Daniel's vision of four beasts rising from the sea, the Old Testament prototype of John's apocalyptic beast.
